content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
John E. Cavanaugh, Esq. 
Paramount City Attorney 
303 West Katella Avenue #272 
Orange, CA 92867 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-17-166 
Dear Mr. Cavanaugh: 
July 28, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Paramount Mayor Peggy 
Lemons regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please 
note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), 
and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Because your question is 
general in nature and not concerning a specific, upcoming governmental decision, we are treating 
your request as one for informal assistance. 2 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Mayor Lemons from taking part in a governmental decision relating 
to a business that is a member of the Paramount Chamber of Commerce given that the Mayor is the 
Executive Director of the Chamber? 
CONCLUSION 
Under the general facts you have provided, the Act would not prohibit the Mayor from 
taking part in a decision impacting a business that is a member of the Chamber solely due to that 
business being a member of the Chamber. However, because definitive advice will depend on the 
specific facts surrounding a future decision, you should contact us for further advice at the time that 
such a decision arises. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
1 Informal assistance does not provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3 ).) 


FACTS 
File No. 1-17-166 
Page No. 2 
You are the Paramount City Attorney and the authorized representative of Mayor Peggy 
Lemons. In her private capacity, the Mayor is employed by the Paramount Chamber of Commerce 
as its Executive Director. 
The Chamber is a nonprofit organization that seeks to facilitate networking among business 
people and civic leaders within the Paramount community. The Chamber provides a wide array of 
infonnation, resources, and services to its members, including access to its Business Resource 
Center. The Chamber also publishes and distributes "Pulse Beat," an award-winning monthly 
newspaper dedicated to promoting local businesses with a circulation of more than 20,000, and the 
annual "Paramount Chamber of Commerce Business and Residential Directory." 
The Chamber's membership includes 275 businesses, and its total budget for fiscal year 
2017 is $309,544.00. The Chamber generates revenue by offering annual memberships and 
sponsorship opportunities. Required investments for annual memberships vary based on whether 
the prospective member is a business, civic organization or utility company, and on the prospective 
member's number of employees if it is a business. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in 
that section. Of those interests, the facts presented implicate the following: 
• An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more provided or promised to, or 
received by, the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The Mayor has a 
source-of-income interest in the Chamber because she is employed as its Executive Director. 
However, under the facts presented, you do not have an interest in any particular member of the 
Chamber.3 
• An interest in the official's personal finances or those of an immediate family member. (See 
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 
Foreseeability & Materiality 
A financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a decision before the official or the official's agency. 
(Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, pennit, or other entitlement to, or 
3 However, we note that the Mayor would have an interest in a Chamber member if the Mayor received 
commission or incentive income with respect to that member joining or remaining a member of the Chamber. The facts 
presented provide no indication that the Mayor receives commission or incentive income. 


File No. 1-17-166 
Page No. 3 
contract with, the interest. (Ibid.) You have not asked about situations where the Chamber will be 
an applicant, so we do not further address this scenario. 
For an interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the decision's financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b).) An effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable. (Ibid.) Under the facts presented, the Chamber would be subject to this rule in cases 
where a member of the Chamber appears before the Mayor. 
Source of Income 
With respect to income received by an official for the provision of services in the ordinary 
course of business, such as the Mayor's income received from the Chamber, Regulation 
18702.3(a)(3) provides that the decision's effect on the official's source-of-income interest is 
material if: (1) the source is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss, or 
(2) the official knows or has reason to know that the nonprofit has a real-property interest that will 
be financially affected under the materiality standards applicable to a real-property interest set forth 
in Regulation 18702.2.4 
Regulation 18702.3(c) sets forth the nexus test, an alternative materiality standard 
potentially applicable to an official's source-of-income interest. The rationale for the nexus test is 
that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or 
she does as an official, we presume that the private employer is benefiting from the actions of the 
employee in his or her official capacity. (Torres Advice Letter, No. 17-117; Todorov Advice Letter, 
No. 16-020; Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) However, the applicability of the nexus test has 
always been premised on the fact that the decision will have a financial effect on the source. 
(Todorov Advice Letter, supra; Maltbie Advice Letter, No. A-15-243; see Hooper Advice Letter, 
No. 1-11-100.) Therefore, the nexus test would not be applicable to a decision's financial effect on 
one or more members of the Chamber unless that decision would also have a financial effect on the 
Chamber. 
In most cases, it would appear that a decision affecting a member of the Chamber will not 
have a financial effect on the Chamber and neither of these standards would apply. However, this is 
a factual determination that must be made once a specific, prospective decision has been identified. 
For example, the Chamber could be foreseeably financially affected if, at the time of the decision, 
the Mayor: ( 1) was soliciting the business to become a member of the Chamber or a Chamber 
sponsor, and the business has not yet responded to the solicitation; or (2) the business has implied 
that the decision's effect may influence whether the business may join or rem_ain a member of the 
Chamber or become or remain a Chamber sponsor. Under such circumstances, the decisions would 
have would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Chamber. (See e.g., 
House Advice Letter, No. I-13-016.) 
4 You have not asked about any real-property interest of the Chamber, so we do not further address this test. 


Personal Finances 
File No. 1-17-166 
Page No. 4 
Regulation 18702.5 provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an 
official's personal finances or those of an immediate family member is material if the official or an 
immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision. For 
example, a decision relating to a Chamber member would have a personal financial effect on the 
Mayor if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision may lead to a reduction in her compensation, 
or her tennination, from the Chamber. 
In summary, the Act would prohibit the Mayor from taking part in a decision relating to a 
Chamber member, only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the Chamber, or the Mayor, or an 
immediate family member of the Mayor will receive a measurable benefit or loss from that 
decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
M 
Counsel, Legal Division
